coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots prepared by gaseous detonation toward excellent friction-reducing and antiwear additives
coredata.dc:creator: He, Chuang
Yan, Honghao
Wang, Xiaohong
Bai, Minli
coredata.dc:description: A great deal of research has been focused on the photoluminescence (PL) behaviors of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), but tribological characteristics against GQDs still remain an open issue. Herein, four kinds of powdery GQDs are successfully synthesized via a one-pot gaseous detonation method without any post-treatment or purification. The tribological properties of GQDs serving as lubricant additives for 150SN mineral oil are investigated in detail using a four-ball tester by altering the concentration and types of GQDs. The results demonstrate that GQDs can act as excellent performance additives. In particular, the mean friction coefficient, wear scar diameter and depth of lower balls lubricated by 150SN mineral oil under 392 N correspondingly reduce by 65.2%, 43.5% and 90.9%, when appropriate types of GQDs at optimal additive concentration of 0.8 wt% are introduced. Finally, on the basis of microstructures, compositions and tribological properties of four kinds of GQDs, a reasonable lubricating mechanism of GQDs-based additives is proposed and illustrated that excellent friction-reducing and antiwear properties may be attributed to the synergetic effects, including polishing effect and mending effect.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Tribological properties
Lubricating mechanism
Gaseous detonation
Photoluminescence behaviors

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of four kinds of GQDs
Tribological properties of G-1 at various concentrations as additives
Tribological properties of G-t (where t = 2, 3, 4) at 0.8 wt% concentration
Surface analyses of the wear scar
Friction-reducing and antiwear mechanism of GQDs-based additives

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the preparation of lubricant suspension.
TEM and HRTEM images of G-1 (a and e), G-2 (b and f), G-3 (c and g), G-4 (d and h), with the corresponding size distribution of GQDs inserted in (a–d).
FT-IR spectra of BA and GQDs.
(a) Friction coefficient curves and (b) mean friction coefficient of G-1 at various concentrations (load: 392 N; speed: 1200 rpm; duration: 60 min; temperature 75 °C).

(a) The wear scar diameters (the red line) and depth (the blue line) of G-1at different content and (b) the viscosity of G-1 at various concentrations. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The mean friction coefficient (a) and wear scar diameters (the red line) and depth (the blue line) (b) of four kinds of GQDs at 0.8 wt% content (load: 392 N; speed: 1200 rpm; duration: 60 min; temperature 75 °C). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
SEM images and Carbon element for center area of the wear scar of the lower ball: 150SN mineral oil (c and b); 0.8 wt% G-3 (c and d).
Schematic illustration of the lubricating mechanism of GQDs-based additives.
Synthesis conditions of four kinds of GQDs.
The differences of four kinds of GQDs.0.99, 0.94, 0.96 and 0.95.
The tribological characteristics of other additives derived from other materials (load: 392 N; speed: 1200 rpm).

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots prepared by gaseous detonation toward excellent friction-reducing and antiwear additives

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Engineering Mechanics, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Energy and Power Engineering, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Energy and Power Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A great deal of research has been focused on the photoluminescence (PL) behaviors of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), but tribological characteristics against GQDs still remain an open issue. Herein, four kinds of powdery GQDs are successfully synthesized via a one-pot gaseous detonation method without any post-treatment or purification. The tribological properties of GQDs serving as lubricant additives for 150SN mineral oil are investigated in detail using a four-ball tester by altering the concentration and types of GQDs. The results demonstrate that GQDs can act as excellent performance additives. In particular, the mean friction coefficient, wear scar diameter and depth of lower balls lubricated by 150SN mineral oil under 392 N correspondingly reduce by 65.2%, 43.5% and 90.9%, when appropriate types of GQDs at optimal additive concentration of 0.8 wt% are introduced. Finally, on the basis of microstructures, compositions and tribological properties of four kinds of GQDs, a reasonable lubricating mechanism of GQDs-based additives is proposed and illustrated that excellent friction-reducing and antiwear properties may be attributed to the synergetic effects, including polishing effect and mending effect.

ce:section.ce:para: In view of significant mechanical loss and resource waste caused by mechanical friction and wear, considerable attention has been paid to the development of lubrication and wear repair materials [1–3]. Over the last decades, carbon nanoparticles, such as fullerene [4,5], carbon nanotubes [6,7], nanodiamonds [8,9], graphene [10,11], carbon dots (CDs) [12–14] and their hybrids [15–17], have been used as additives in tribology area. Among them, CDs being a new kind of carbon materials have extensively investigated as lubricant additives due to favorable dispersibility, small size effect and functionalized surfaces. Recently, Kang et al. [12] has report a simple method to obtain CDs/CuSx nanocomposites toward high efficient lubrication and metal wear repair. Wang et al. [13] and Cai et al. [14] have successfully synthesized CDs/ionic liquids nanocomposites as an efficient lubrication additive. The nanocomposites containing CDs have exhibited excellent lubricating properties. Despite tremendous progress has been done for CDs-based additives, the application is severely limited on account of a complex process to obtain powdery CDs [12–14]. At the same time, the lubricating mechanism remains to be elucidated. Hence, it's desirable to develop high efficient and available nanomaterials and further research tribological mechanism.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) emerging as promising zero-dimensional carbon based nanoparticles with less than 100 nm in lateral dimensions exhibit outstanding physicochemical properties, such as photoluminescence, low toxicity and environmental friendliness [18,19], which is comparable to CDs. For all we know, the application of GQDs in lubrication has not been exploited. According to tribological properties of CDs-based additives, GQDs should possess high performance in tribological communities because of uniform and small size, high specific surface area and tunable surfaces. Moreover, if the limitations of GQDs, such as complex synthetic approaches and time-consuming processes, are overcome, GQDs-based additives will become a more promising choice. Therefore, there remains highly desired and urgent necessary to develop a facile method to prepare GQDs and explore the tribological properties and lubrication mechanism of GQDs-based additives. Our prior work has demonstrated that the gaseous detonation approach using hydrogen and oxygen as explosion sources to generate detonation wave acted with untreated benzoic acid (BA) precursor can prepare powdery GQDs directly in 3–5 ms with large-scale fabrication, time-saving process and high reproducibility without any post-treatment or purification [20]. As far as we know, the gaseous detonation method has been the most rapid and convenient pathway to produce powdery GQDs directly so far.

Herein, four types of GQDs are obtained by altering the molar ratio of BA, hydrogen and oxygen during gaseous detonation. The tribological properties of GQDs serving as lubricant additives for 150SN mineral oil are investigated in detail using a four-ball tester by altering the concentration and types of GQDs. The results demonstrate that GQDs can serve as a novel and high performance lubricant additive. In particular, the mean friction coefficient, wear scar diameter and depth of lower balls lubricated by 150SN mineral oil under 392 N correspondingly reduce by 65.2%, 43.5% and 90.9%, when appropriate types of GQDs at optimal additive concentration of 0.8 wt% are introduced. To the best of our knowledge, the GQDs-based lubricant additives with the mean friction coefficient of 0.031 under 392 N have displayed the best friction-reducing property until now. Finally, based on the microstructures, compositions and tribological properties of four kinds of GQDs, a reasonable friction-reducing and antiwear mechanism of GQDs-based additives is proposed.

Benzoic acid (BA, AR grade) was obtained from Shanghai Sinopharm Chemical Regent Co., Ltd. (China). Hydrogen and oxygen (>99.999%) were purchased from Dalian Special Gases Co., Ltd. (China). 150SN mineral oil without any additive was employed as lube base oil. All chemicals were used as received without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of four kinds of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The GQDs used in the experiment were synthesized by gaseous detonation using BA as the carbon source as well as hydrogen and oxygen as the explosion source. Firstly, BA was added to a self-made 8.6 L detonation titanium tube (Fig. S1). Then the tube was vacuumized and heated to 65 °C. Thirdly, the gas mixture was detonated after hydrogen and oxygen were pumped into the tube with a certain proportion. Finally, when the tube cooled down to 60 °C, black powdery GQDs were collected. Table 1 sets out the experiment parameters. More experimental details can be seen in our previous work [20].

The lubricant containing GQDs-based additives was prepared by adding certain amounts of the as-obtained products into 150SN mineral oil at room temperature under ultrasonic agitation for 30 min, as illustrated in Fig. 1 .

High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were recorded using a Tecnai F20 transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were measured on a MultiMode3D atomic force microscopy (Veeco, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were confirmed by an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku D/MAX 2400, Japan) using Cu Kα radiation. Raman spectra were performed by a DXR microscope Raman spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained using a Lambda 750 UV–vis-NIR spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were characterized by a Nicolet-6700 FTIR spectrophotometer (Thermo, USA). PL spectra were recorded by a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi High Technologies, Japan). Elemental analyses were performed by the Vario EL elemental analyzer (Element, Germany). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were acquired using a FEI-quanta 450 scanning electron microscope (FEI, USA) equipped with Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectrometer (EDS) analyses.

The lubricant viscosity was measured by a DV-II+ pro viscometers (Brookfield, USA). The friction and wear tests were conducted on a MMW-1A microcomputer control friction and wear tester (Jinan Yihua Tribology Testing Technology Co., Ltd. China) through rotating an AISI52100 steel ball with 12.7 mm in diameter and hardness of 59–61 HRC over three identical balls, i.e. a four-ball model under the following conditions: rotating rate, 1200 rpm; duration, 60 min; load, 392 N; and temperature, 75 °C. Three-dimensional (3D) images and surface roughness of lower balls were obtained by an OLS4000 confocal laser scanning microscopy (Olympus, Japan), and friction coefficients were recorded simultaneously by a computer linked to the tester.

The images of samples (Fig. S2) reveal that G-4 contains multitudes of white crystals besides black powder while the others only contain black powder. TEM images are shown in Fig. 2a–d, suggesting G-1, G-3 and G-4 are well-dispersed with average sizes of 6.3, 5.6 and 2.0 nm respectively while G-2 have obvious agglomeration with an average diameter of 28.3 nm. Their HRTEM images (Fig. 2e–h) indicate all the samples are crystalline with spacings of 0.21 or 0.34 nm, corresponding to the graphene (100) or graphite (001) planes [21,22], which indicates all the four samples are GQDs. It should be noted that GQDs in G-1 to G-3 contain multiple sp2 subdomains joined through sp3 carbons [23], functional groups or defects. Hence, it is possible for raw GQDs to break down into smaller new GQDs under external forces. As shown in Fig. S3, by-products of G-1 to G-3 are graphite nanosheets with the range of 50–200 nm in size while the coproduct of G-4 is carbon sphere with diameters of over 100 nm. AFM analyses (Fig. S4) illustrate average thicknesses of G-1 to G-4 are approximately 1.2, 2.1, 2.5, and 1.6 nm, respectively, suggesting that multilayered GQDs are obtained [24].

The evidences for the conclusions drawn from products images, TEM and AFM images are provided by XRD and Raman spectra (Fig. S5). XRD patterns of G-1 to G-3 show two broad peaks centered at around 24.6° and 43.4° corresponding to the graphite (002) and graphene (100) planes respectively [21,25,26], indicating they have sp2-hybridized structures. In addition, the fact that XRD patterns of G-4 exhibit multiple high and sharp peaks coinciding with those of BA and covering peaks of the graphite suggests white crystals in G-4 product are BA. The Raman spectra of GQDs show the typical D band at approximately 1345 cm−1 and G band at around 1590 cm−1, corresponding to disordered sp3 defects and the sp2 bonds, respectively [27,28]. The I D/I G ratios of G-1 to G-4 are 0.99, 0.94, 0.96 and 0.95, respectively, implying that all the GQDs have a good degree of crystallinity [20]. It is noteworthy that there is no 2D band at 2700–2900 cm−1, indicating that the all the GQDs possess multilayered structures which are beneficial to good lubrication [16].

FT-IR spectra (Fig. 3 ) are measured to investigate the chemical structure change of BA during detonation, indicating all the GQDs have more abundant groups than BA, which endow GQDs with better dispersion in 150SN mineral oil. Compared with that of BA, two distinct new bonds in the vicinity of 1090 and 3425 cm−1 appear in all the GQDs, belonging to COC and OH, respectively [28–30]. Meanwhile, the peaks at 2850 and 2922 cm−1 in all the GQDs prove the existence of CH [31,32]. The peak at approximately 1600 cm−1 rising from CC can be found in all the GQDs [33].

Elemental analyses (Table S1) are carried out to disclose composition of GQDs. Due to containing BA, G-4 has the maximum oxygen content. As for G-1 to G-3, the summation content of oxygen and hydrogen is under 10%, suggesting BA goes through a sequencial deoxidized condensation and dehydrogenative planarization approach to form GQDs. According to oxygen content of G-1 to G-3, it is obvious that G-3 has the most oxygen-containing groups, then G-1, and finally G-2.

The UV–vis spectra (Fig. S6) of G-1 to G-3 exhibit two main absorption bands at 251 and 287 nm derived from π–π* transition of aromatic sp2 subdomains [34], and multiple minor peaks over 300 nm assigned to n–π* absorption of oxygen-containing functional groups [35]. And G-4 displays a strong absorption band at 225 nm same to BA. The PL spectra of GQDs show wavelength-independent emission with the maximum emission at 460 nm derived from sp2 subdomains [20]. The GQDs dispersed in ethyl alcohol are yellow in daylight and emit blue light under UV irradiation (Fig. S7). The differences of four samples are shown in Table 2 .

All the results drawn from characterization prove that all the GQDs prepared by gaseous detonation possess multilayered structures and multitudes of sp2 subdomains with oxygen-containing functional groups. In addition, G-4 consists of some BA and by-product of lager carbon spheres due to the excessive precursor, which may be against lubrication.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tribological properties of G-1 at various concentrations as additives
ce:section.ce:para: The dispersibility of the lubrication additive play an important role in tribological applications [36]. Fig. S8 shows the optical photographs of G-1/150SN mineral oil suspension at different concentrations exposure to sunlight and UV irradiation, indicating that GQDs dissolve uniformly and possess good dispersion in 150SN mineral oil. The TEM image (Fig. S9) of G-1 at content of 0.8 wt% demonstrates that compared with dispersing G-1 in ethyl alcohol (Fig. 2a), G-1 dispersed in 150SN mineral oil is more uniform and has a smaller average diameter of 2.5 nm, agreeing with the presumption that the sp2 subdomain being a part of raw GQDs easily break down into smaller new GQDs under external forces which may come from alkyl chains of 150SN mineral oil under ultrasonic agitation.

To evaluate the tribological performance of GQDs-based additives, c wt% G-1 (where c = 0, 0.05, 0.5, 0.8, 1, 2) have been tested using a four-ball model. The friction coefficient curves and the mean friction coefficient of G-1 at different concentration are shown in Fig. 4 . It can be seen that the friction coefficient curves of 0 wt% G-1 (i.e., 150SN mineral oil) are unstable and increase sharply to 0.27 at the start, while all the curves are smooth when G-1 is added (Fig. 4a). The mean friction coefficient decreases drastically with the increment of G-1, and it is lowest when G-1 at concentration of 0.8 wt% is added (Fig. 4b). As more G-1 is introduced, the mean friction coefficient slightly increases and then remains almost constant. It is clear that G-1 as additives can enhance significantly friction-reducing performance. Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. 5a, both the wear scar diameter and depth sharing a similar downtrend significantly decrease with the concentration of G-1 increasing. The images of wear scar diameter and 3D images of wear scar are displayed in Fig. S10. The lowest wear scar diameter and depth are simultaneously obtained at 2.0 wt% content which is different from the content of lowest mean friction coefficient. The possible reason can be explained by the different viscosity. As displayed in Fig. 5b, the viscosity at 75 °C increases with the increase of G-1. It is well known that the viscosity has a major impact on the thickness of the lubricant film and friction coefficient [37,38]. The higher concentration of G-1 leading to a bigger viscosity and friction coefficient due to agglomeration and precipitation, however, is advantageous to increase the thickness of the lubricating film between the rubbing surfaces reducing the wear scar diameters and depth. On the contrary, lubricants with lower viscosity are difficult to form a lubricating film, which may have a smaller friction coefficient but aggravate wear. It is clear that G-1 as additives has significant tribological performance, and the optimal content of G-1 for 150SN mineral oil is 0.8 wt%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tribological properties of G-t (where t = 2, 3, 4) at 0.8 wt% concentration
ce:section.ce:para: Further, the tribological properties of 0.8 wt% G-t have been tested. Only G-4 shows the distinct increasing friction coefficient with time (Fig. S11), meaning G-4 may have agglomeration under high pressure [39]. As shown in Fig. 6a, the lowest mean friction coefficient belonging to 0.8 wt% G-3 is 0.031, meaning 65% improvement than that of 150SN mineral oil. To the best of our knowledge, prior lubricant additives have greatly reduced the friction coefficient, but there is few additives decrease mean friction coefficient to 0.031 at optimal additive concentrations under the same experimental conditions. In other words, it is the GQDs-based additives reported here that display a better friction-reducing property. Fig. 6b shows the wear scar diameter and depth of four types of GQDs at 0.8 wt% concentration and the corresponding images of wear scar diameter and 3D images of wear scar are shown in Fig. S12. It can be seen that 0.8 wt% G-3 has smaller wear scar diameter and depth. As listed in Table 3 , we have made a survey about tribological characteristics of the reported lubricant additives. Compared with the additives derived from other materials, the GQDs prepared by gaseous detonation not only avoid complex post-treatment, but also have better friction-reducing performance.

It should be noted that G-4 and G-1 possess the minimum and maximum viscosity, respectively (Fig. S13). The reason why G-3, not the others, exhibits superior lubrication performance can be explained by characterization mentioned above. The carbon spheres over 100 nm and BA contained in G-4 are disadvantage to lubrication by virtue of the fact that large carbon spheres and BA not readily soluble in 150SN mineral oil tend to agglomeration and then restrain the formation of lubricating film. On the contrary, graphite sheets as by-products endow G-1 to G-3 with excellent friction-reducing performance [16]. In other words, the microstructure rather than viscosity is a predominant factor in friction-reducing performance. At the same time, G-4 with lower viscosity is hard to form lubricant film, leading to larger wear scar diameter. For G-3, besides favorable microstructures and compositions, more oxygen-containing functional groups leading to a better dispersion are also beneficial to lubrication. In addition, owing to particles aggregating, it is disadvantageous for G-2 to fill the deep scratches. Therefore, G-2 has deeper wear scar depth than G-1 and G-3, being second to G-4.

All the results demonstrate that GQDs obtained by gaseous detonation with small size, multiple layers and massive groups, especially G-3 at concentration of 0.8 wt%, exhibit excellent friction-reducing and antiwear properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface analyses of the wear scar
ce:section.ce:para: Generally speaking, a uniform lubricating film between the rubbing surfaces plays a leading role in reducing the friction and wear under boundary lubrication conditions [12]. As a result, it is necessary to understand morphology and carbon element distribution of wear scar. SEM and EDS (Fig. 7 ) have been employed to characterize the wear scar washed by absolute alcohol. The wear scar of 0.8 wt% G-3 (Fig. 7c and d) possesses more dark color active areas and higher carbon concentration than those of 150SN mineral oil (Fig. 7a and b), meaning GQDs can attach easily on the worn surface to form a lubricating film thus fulfilling the better tribological properties against friction and wear.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Friction-reducing and antiwear mechanism of GQDs-based additives
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the results stated above, a reasonable lubricating mechanism of GQDs-based additives is proposed and illustrated that excellent friction-reducing and outstanding antiwear properties are attributed to the synergetic effects, including polishing effect and mending effect. It is well known that during the course of friction, positive charges are formed at contact convex points between the upper steel ball and the lower steel ball after emitting the low-energy electrons [13]. As shown in Fig. 8 , GQDs tend to attach to the worn surface which is full of positive charges owing to electrostatic attraction. Consequently, a lubricating film containing GQDs with multiple layers can be formed, which not only leads to smaller wear scar diameter, but also realizes lower friction coefficient than that of 150SN mineral oil owing to the sliding and rolling effect of GQDs, which is similar to polishing effect. It should be noted that the appropriate content of GQDs have a close relation with the friction coefficient and thickness of lubricating film. With prolongation of test time, some rupture can occur in the lubricating film, which can be seen through fluctuant friction coefficient curves in Fig. S11. Subsequently, intensive tribochemical reaction should occur via the active groups of GQDs between shear surfaces and lubricating film [46]. As a result, GQDs with more active groups is easier to attach to the worn surface and possess smaller size and better dispersibility in favor of superior mending effect by filling the deep scratches, which explain that G-3 has better anti-wear performances than G-1 and G-2. Last but not least, the effect of coproducts on lubrication, causing G-4 possess the worst lubrication among GQDs, should not be ignored. Graphite sheets may assemble on the rubbing surface, being more difficult to be fully squeezed out of the contact area than carbon spheres due to their relatively large surface area, which makes for lubrication [16].

The microstructures, compositions and tribological properties of four kinds of powdery GQDs obtained by the gaseous detonation method are researched. The as-prepared GQDs without any purification can serve as a novel and high performance lubricant additive. Most important, GQDs with smaller sizes, better dispersion and more oxygen-containing groups display more excellent lubricating performance. Finally, a reasonable lubricating mechanism of GQDs-based additives is proposed and illustrated that excellent friction-reducing and antiwear properties may be attributed to the synergetic effects, including polishing effect and mending effect. In a word, all the results demonstrate that GQDs can act as excellent performance additives and find wide applications in mechanical protection and resource saving.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The project was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 11672068, 10872044, and 11672067).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2018.09.019.
